title: Chocolate Ice Cream
subtitle: Dark chocolate custard base churned to a rich, dense ice cream
excerpt: A custard base made with dark chocolate and cocoa powder, cooked to a thick anglaise, churned in an ice cream maker. Richer and denser than vanilla because the chocolate itself provides body alongside the custard. Very dark in flavour.
sourceNotes: Chocolate ice cream developed as a variation of the French custard-based ice cream tradition, adding dark chocolate and cocoa powder to the anglaise base. The combination of chocolate and cocoa in a custard base became standard in European patisserie and gelato making in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

recipe.freezeNotes: Store in a lidded freezer-proof container for up to one month. Transfer to the fridge for 15 minutes before serving to soften.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The custard base can be made up to two days ahead and refrigerated. Churn when needed.

content.text: The chocolate does two things in this ice cream: it flavours the custard and it thickens it. The cocoa powder adds depth and bitterness beyond what the chocolate alone provides. Use chocolate with at least 70% cocoa solids, the flavour of a lower-cocoa bar gets diluted in the custard and produces a mild, sweet result rather than an intensely chocolate one.

The process is the same as vanilla ice cream: make a custard, chill it completely, churn it. The main difference is that the melted chocolate goes into the hot custard at the end, thickening it further. This ice cream sets firmer than vanilla and may need a few more minutes out of the freezer before scooping.
Whisk {{cocoa-powder}} of cocoa powder into {{whole-milk}} of whole milk in a saucepan until smooth. Add {{double-cream}} of double cream. Heat over medium heat until just below simmering.

Whisk 5 egg yolks with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar until pale. Pour the warm cocoa cream over the yolks in a slow stream, whisking constantly. Return to the saucepan and cook over low heat, stirring continuously, for 8, 12 minutes until the custard thickens and coats the back of the spoon. Remove from the heat and add {{dark-chocolate}} of finely chopped dark chocolate. Stir until the chocolate is fully melted and the custard is smooth. Strain through a fine sieve into a bowl. Cool completely, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours.

Churn the cold custard in an ice cream maker until it reaches a soft, scoopable consistency. Transfer to a lidded freezer-proof container and freeze for at least 2 hours. Allow 20 minutes at room temperature before scooping, chocolate ice cream sets firmer than vanilla and needs slightly longer to soften.

items.symptom: Custard has lumps of cooked chocolate
items.cause: Chocolate added to custard that was too cool, or chocolate seized
items.fix: Add the chocolate to the custard while it is still hot from the hob. Stir until completely smooth. Straining through a sieve will remove any lumps.

items.symptom: Ice cream is very hard and won't scoop
items.cause: High cocoa content makes chocolate ice cream set firmer
items.fix: Transfer to the fridge for 20 minutes before scooping. Alternatively, dip the scoop in hot water before each scoop.

items.symptom: Flavour is weak and sweet rather than intense
items.cause: Chocolate with less than 70% cocoa, or not enough cocoa powder
items.fix: Use 70% chocolate and measure the full amount of cocoa powder. A lower-cocoa chocolate simply cannot produce the same depth of flavour.

items.symptom: Ice cream has ice crystals throughout
items.cause: Custard was not cold enough before churning
items.fix: Chill the custard overnight before churning. The ice cream maker bowl must also be fully frozen according to the manufacturer's instructions.

content.text: Chocolate ice cream developed alongside vanilla as one of the two foundational flavours of European custard-based ice cream. The combination of cocoa powder and chocolate in the custard base was standard in French and Italian patisserie by the nineteenth century. In Britain, chocolate ice cream became widely available as the ice cream industry industrialised in the early twentieth century, though the bought version typically uses far less cocoa than a proper custard-based preparation. The homemade version is substantially more intense.
